text: blaKPC (Klebsiella pneumoniae carbapenemase, e.g. KPC-2, KPC-3) is a class A serine carbapenemase that hydrolyses carbapenems, penicillins, and cephalosporins. Its presence is a strong, near-specific marker of carbapenem resistance in K. pneumoniae and also confers ceftriaxone resistance.
source: curated: antibiotic-panel.md; NCBI Reference Gene Catalog; CARD ARO

text: blaNDM (New Delhi metallo-beta-lactamase, e.g. NDM-1, NDM-5) is a class B metallo-carbapenemase requiring zinc that hydrolyses virtually all beta-lactams except aztreonam, including carbapenems and cephalosporins. NDM is not inhibited by classical serine-beta-lactamase inhibitors.
source: curated: antibiotic-panel.md; NCBI Reference Gene Catalog; CARD ARO

text: blaOXA-48-like (e.g. OXA-48, OXA-181, OXA-232) is a class D carbapenemase that weakly hydrolyses carbapenems and penicillins with little cephalosporin activity; carbapenem MICs can be low, so OXA-48 carriers may be phenotypically borderline yet clinically resistant.
source: curated: antibiotic-panel.md; NCBI Reference Gene Catalog; CARD ARO

text: blaVIM and blaIMP are class B metallo-carbapenemases (like NDM) that hydrolyse carbapenems and most beta-lactams. They are acquired on integrons/plasmids and confer carbapenem resistance.
source: curated: antibiotic-panel.md; NCBI Reference Gene Catalog; CARD ARO

text: Loss or disruption of the OmpK35/OmpK36 outer-membrane porins reduces carbapenem uptake. Combined with an ESBL or AmpC this yields carbapenem resistance even without a carbapenemase; porin loss alone lowers susceptibility but is not deterministic, so a carbapenemase-negative genotype does not guarantee susceptibility.

blaCTX-M (e.g. CTX-M-15, CTX-M-14) is the dominant extended-spectrum beta-lactamase (ESBL) family worldwide and efficiently hydrolyses 3rd-generation cephalosporins such as ceftriaxone and cefotaxime. It does not hydrolyse carbapenems.
source: curated: antibiotic-panel.md; NCBI Reference Gene Catalog; CARD ARO

text: Extended-spectrum blaSHV variants (e.g. SHV-12) confer cephalosporin resistance, unlike the narrow-spectrum chromosomal blaSHV-1/blaSHV-11 which do not. AMRFinderPlus Subclass distinguishes ESBL (CEPHALOSPORIN) from narrow-spectrum (BETA-LACTAM) variants.
source: curated: antibiotic-panel.md; NCBI Reference Gene Catalog

text: Extended-spectrum blaTEM variants hydrolyse 3rd-generation cephalosporins; the parental TEM-1/TEM-2 are narrow-spectrum penicillinases. Only the ESBL variants are relevant to ceftriaxone resistance.
Acquired AmpC beta-lactamases such as blaCMY hydrolyse cephalosporins and, unlike ESBLs, are not inhibited by clavulanate. AmpC hyperproduction or plasmid-borne CMY confers ceftriaxone resistance.

Point mutations in the quinolone-resistance-determining region (QRDR) of gyrA (DNA gyrase A, typically codons S83 and D87) reduce fluoroquinolone binding. A single QRDR mutation lowers susceptibility; combinations of gyrA plus parC mutations produce high-level ciprofloxacin resistance.

QRDR mutations in parC (topoisomerase IV, typically codons S80 and E84) contribute to fluoroquinolone resistance, usually additively with gyrA mutations. Ciprofloxacin resistance scales with the number of accumulated target-site mutations.

qnr genes (qnrA, qnrB, qnrS) are plasmid-mediated quinolone-resistance (PMQR) determinants that protect DNA gyrase/topoisomerase IV from fluoroquinolones. They typically confer low-level resistance that can combine with QRDR mutations for a higher-level phenotype.

aac(6')-Ib-cr is a bifunctional variant of the aac(6')-Ib aminoglycoside acetyltransferase that additionally acetylates ciprofloxacin, lowering its activity. It is treated as a plasmid-mediated quinolone-resistance determinant for ciprofloxacin.
oqxAB and qepA encode efflux pumps that export fluoroquinolones, reducing ciprofloxacin susceptibility as plasmid-mediated quinolone-resistance determinants.

16S rRNA methyltransferases (armA, rmtB, rmtC, rmtD) methylate the aminoglycoside binding site on the 30S ribosome, conferring high-level resistance to essentially all clinically used aminoglycosides including gentamicin. Their presence is a near-binary, strong predictor of gentamicin resistance.
source: curated: antibiotic-panel.md; NCBI Reference Gene Catalog; CARD ARO

text: aac(3) aminoglycoside N-acetyltransferases (e.g. aac(3)-II) acetylate gentamicin and related 4,6-disubstituted deoxystreptamines, conferring gentamicin resistance. Substrate range is enzyme-specific, so the phenotypic effect is moderate and drug-dependent rather than universal.

Other aminoglycoside-modifying enzymes (aac(6')-Ib, aph(3')-VI, ant(2'')-Ia) have substrate ranges that variably include gentamicin; aac(6')-Ib primarily affects amikacin/tobramycin. These are statistical, drug-specific signals rather than deterministic gentamicin markers.

sul1, sul2 and sul3 encode drug-insensitive dihydropteroate synthases that bypass sulfonamide inhibition, conferring sulfamethoxazole resistance. They account for the large majority of sulfonamide resistance and often co-occur with dfr genes on class-1 integrons.

dfrA-family genes (e.g. dfrA1, dfrA12, dfrA17) encode trimethoprim-insensitive dihydrofolate reductases that bypass trimethoprim inhibition, conferring trimethoprim resistance. Combined sul + dfr carriage strongly predicts trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole resistance.

Narrow-spectrum chromosomal blaSHV-1/blaSHV-11 (AMRFinderPlus Subclass BETA-LACTAM) are intrinsic penicillinases in K. pneumoniae and do NOT hydrolyse 3rd-generation cephalosporins; they must not be counted as ESBLs when reasoning about ceftriaxone.
